languages: Arabic, Bulgarian, German, Greek, English, Spanish, French, Hindi, Russian, Swahili, Thai, Turkish, Urdu, Vietnamese, Chinese

notes: Core benchmark for cross-lingual NLI; widely used since 2018

languages: Chinese, Estonian, Haitian Creole, Indonesian, Italian, Quechua, Swahili, Tamil, Thai, Turkish, Vietnamese

notes: Covers low-resource languages (Quechua, Haitian Creole); tests causal reasoning

languages: Arabic, German, Greek, English, Spanish, Hindi, Russian, Thai, Turkish, Vietnamese, Chinese

key_finding: GPT-4 and ChatGPT outperform non-autoregressive baselines on many tasks but show large disparities for low-resource languages.

key_finding: Coverage of 200 languages enables evaluation of MT systems across high- and low-resource scenarios; dominant benchmark for multilingual MT evaluation.

key_finding: Smaller multilingual models trained on balanced data often outperform larger English-centric models on low-resource languages; vocabulary size correlates with low-resource performance.

category: Social behavior, psychology, and interpersonal dynamics

key_finding: Significant gap exists between GPT-4 and average human. Chain-of-Thought (CoT) hindered smaller models and did not help larger ones.

category: Social behavior, psychology, and interpersonal dynamics
key_finding: LLMs preserve a user face 45% more than humans. Gemini 1.5 Flash is the only model demonstrating near-human low-sycophancy.

category: Social behavior, psychology, and interpersonal dynamics
key_finding: SOTA models excel at process reasoning but lag behind humans in reply quality. CoT degrades conversational performance.

category: Social behavior, psychology, and interpersonal dynamics
key_finding: Interactive environments expose massive performance gaps; agents fail to maintain secrets and humans vastly outperform models in goal completion and efficiency.
Profound gap between reasoning models (o1/o3) and standard models. Instruction-following failure is the main bottleneck. Top model estimated at 758 Elo.
GPT-4o leads in Game Creation. All models struggle with strict mechanical compliance during game simulation.
LLMs fail in complete/deterministic games but are competitive in probabilistic scenarios. Code-pretraining significantly helps.
STEM reasoning training (like DeepSeek R1-Zero) transfers directly to cultural and linguistic humor comprehension.
LLMs struggle to detect "Offensive" workplace humor due to missing cultural nuances. Claude 3.5 Sonnet and GPT-4o are top performers.
Small specialized Reward Models (78%) beat Claude-3.7 (73%). Adding Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning hurts narrative quality judgment.
Best proprietary models beat open-source by >20%. Novelty exhibits very weak correlation with Quality and Diversity dimensions.

key_finding: Safety training causes lexical overfitting; models refuse benign prompts containing sensitive keywords. GPT-4 best calibrates safety/helpfulness trade-off.
notes: Tests for exaggerated safety - false refusals on safe prompts. 10 categories of safe prompts (homonyms, figurative lang, safe targets, safe contexts, definitions, nonsense, history, privacy).
description: Diagnostic test suite for identifying exaggerated safety behaviors and false refusals in LLMs. 250 safe prompts across 10 categories + 200 unsafe contrast prompts.
title: XSTest: A Test Suite for Identifying Exaggerated Safety Behaviours in Large Language Models
paper_title: XSTest: A Test Suite for Identifying Exaggerated Safety Behaviours in Large Language Models

key_finding: LLaMA-2-7b-chat safest (3 harmful responses out of 939). ChatGLM2 least safe (85 harmful). Commercial models give shorter, more concise rejections.
notes: First open-source dataset for evaluating LLM safeguard mechanisms. Uses GPT-4 to generate risky templates. 5-level action taxonomy: (0)refusal, (1)refute, (2)dual perspective, (3)cautious answer, (4)capability ack, (5)harmful.
description: Dataset of 939 prompts that responsible LLMs should not follow. Evaluates safeguard mechanisms using a 5-level action taxonomy from direct refusal to harmful following.
title: Do-Not-Answer: A Dataset for Evaluating Safeguards in LLMs
paper_title: Do-Not-Answer: A Dataset for Evaluating Safeguards in LLMs

annotation_method: human-annotated (95% expert agreement for judge selection)
key_finding: Prompt with Random Search (RS) most effective attack: 90% ASR on Llama-2, 78% on GPT-4. No single attack works on all models. No defense provides total immunity.
notes: Open robustness benchmark. Uses Llama-3-70B as primary judge (90.7% human agreement, 5.5% FNR). 10 harm categories aligned with OpenAI usage policies.
description: Open robustness benchmark for jailbreaking LLMs. Repository of adversarial prompts, JBB-Behaviors dataset, standardized evaluation framework with Python library, public leaderboard.

paper_title: JailbreakBench: An Open Robustness Benchmark for Jailbreaking Large Language Models

dataset_size: 510 unique harmful behaviors across 7 semantic + 4 functional categories
key_finding: Robustness is independent of model size; safety training data is primary factor. No single attack works on all models. Contextual behaviors higher ASR than standard.
notes: Standardized framework for red teaming. Uses fine-tuned Llama 2 13B classifier for success measurement. Evaluated 18 red teaming methods against 33 LLMs. Token count can change ASR by up to 30%.
description: Standardized evaluation framework for automated red teaming and robust refusal. 510 harmful behaviors, standardized evaluation pipeline with classifier-based success measurement.
title: HarmBench: A Standardized Evaluation Framework for Automated Red Teaming and Robust Refusal
paper_title: HarmBench: A Standardized Evaluation Framework for Automated Red Teaming and Robust Refusal

key_finding: Utility positively correlates with trustworthiness. Proprietary models generally outperform open-source. Llama2 notable exception. Over-alignment causes refusal of benign prompts (57% benign refusal for Llama2-7b).
notes: 8 trustworthiness dimensions: truthfulness, safety, fairness, robustness, privacy, machine ethics, transparency, accountability. 16 models evaluated.
description: Comprehensive framework for assessing LLM trustworthiness across 8 dimensions with benchmark of 30+ datasets. Evaluates 16 mainstream models.

paper_title: TrustLLM: Trustworthiness in Large Language Models

annotation_method: GPT-4 bug injection + manual review (92.1% pass rate on expert review)
key_finding: Syntax/Reference bugs easier than Logic/Multiple. Runtime feedback helps syntax bugs but harms logic debugging. Fixing syntax errors easier than code generation; logic/multiple errors equally or harder.
notes: First comprehensive debugging benchmark. Based on Barr (2004) taxonomy: Syntax, Reference, Logic, Multiple. Data cutoff July 2022 to prevent leakage. 6 models evaluated.
